YOLO-MDF: A Multi-Domain Fusion Detector for Steel Surface Defect Detection

## 1 Introduction

Steel surface defect detection is critical for quality control in manufacturing. Traditional manual inspection is labor-intensive and error-prone. Recent deep learning methods based on YOLO have shown promise but struggle with small defects and complex backgrounds. We propose YOLO-MDF, a multi-domain fusion detector that combines frequency-domain and spatial-domain features for robust detection. Our approach addresses three key challenges: small defect localization, class imbalance across six defect categories, and false positives under varying illumination.

## 2 Related Work

Prior work on steel surface defect detection can be grouped into three families. Classical image processing methods (edge detection, morphological operations) are fast but fragile under noise. CNN-based two-stage detectors (Faster R-CNN, Cascade R-CNN) achieve high accuracy but lag in real-time. One-stage YOLO variants dominate industrial deployment due to their speed-accuracy balance. NEU-DET is the standard benchmark for this task, containing six defect classes: rolled-in scale (RS), patches (Pa), crazing (Cr), pitted surface (PS), inclusion (In), and scratches (Sc).

## 3 Method

YOLO-MDF extends YOLOv8 with three core modules. First, the Frequency-Aware Block (FAB) applies a learnable DCT to extract high-frequency defect signatures, complementing the spatial stream. Second, the Multi-Scale Aggregation Neck (MSAN) fuses P3, P4, and P5 feature maps via weighted bi-directional paths, improving small defect detection. Third, we replace the standard CIoU loss with SIoU loss, which considers angle and shape similarity, accelerating convergence.

The backbone uses CSPDarknet-53 with cross-stage partial connections. The neck is a modified Path Aggregation Network (PANet) with a small-object detection head at P2. The detection head is anchor-free and predicts class probabilities via sigmoid activation.

## 4 Experiment

We evaluate YOLO-MDF on the NEU-DET benchmark, following the standard 6-fold cross-validation protocol. The dataset contains 1800 images at 200x200 pixels, evenly distributed across six defect classes. We compare against YOLOv5s, YOLOv8n, and Faster R-CNN baselines. All experiments use the same train/val split (1440/360) and are trained for 300 epochs on a single NVIDIA RTX 3090.

Implementation details: batch size 16, SGD optimizer with momentum 0.937 and weight decay 0.0005, initial learning rate 0.01 with cosine annealing, and standard data augmentation (Mosaic, MixUp, random horizontal flip).

## 5 Result

YOLO-MDF achieves 82.3% mAP@0.5 on NEU-DET, outperforming YOLOv8n (76.8%) by 5.5 points and Faster R-CNN (74.1%) by 8.2 points. The improvement is most pronounced for small defects like crazing and inclusions, where FAB and MSAN contribute the most. Inference speed on RTX 3090 reaches 142 FPS, satisfying real-time industrial requirements.

Ablation studies confirm each module's contribution: FAB alone adds 2.1 mAP, MSAN alone adds 1.8 mAP, and SIoU alone adds 0.9 mAP. The full YOLO-MDF combines these gains almost additively, reaching the final 82.3% mAP.

## 6 Conclusion

We presented YOLO-MDF, a multi-domain fusion detector that combines frequency-domain and spatial-domain features for steel surface defect detection. The proposed FAB, MSAN, and SIoU modules together yield a 5.5-point mAP improvement on NEU-DET while maintaining real-time inference speed. Future work will explore transformer-based backbones and weakly supervised training with unlabeled factory images.
